text: In the laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, the technically most difficult step is the dissection of the preparatineal space for a synthetic mesh placement. In this video, we present a technique of dissecting these tissues, allowing to maintain full control of the dissected structures. The presented case is a small medial direct inguinal hernia in a male patient.

The surgeon identifies crucial anatomical structures before incising the peritoneum, including the external iliac artery and vein, the oblique vas deferens connecting with the testicular veins at the deep inguinal ring, and assesses the bladder's position and filling. The assisting surgeon sets the horizon and frames the image appropriately.

The surgeon grasps a thin layer of the peritoneal lamina approximately five to six centimeters laterally from the deep inguinal ring. After pulling it back, a gentle incision is made, allowing gas to enter the preperitoneal space and separate the tissues in the avascular layer. Small visible vessels in the peritoneal lamina are cauterized gently before cutting.

The surgeon pulls the peritoneum away from the abdominal wall, stretching it along the cutting line while detaching loose connective tissue. The incision progresses towards the top of the screen, then slightly medial, and finally cranially before reaching the medial umbilical ligament. Care is taken to avoid damaging the inferior epigastric vessels by pulling the peritoneum on one side and sliding the vessels on the other, ensuring they remain on the rectus muscle.

The surgeon maintains the correct direction of the peritoneal incision while ensuring a distance of at least five to six centimeters from the edge of the hernia defect. The grasper held in the left hand plays a dominant role in tissue dissection, providing solid peritoneal traction at the right angle.

In the lateral part of the dissected space, the surgeon removes connective and fatty tissue from the peritoneum, leaving it attached to the abdominal wall muscles. In the medial part, the technique is reversed, with bare muscle visible at the top of the screen and fatty tissue positioned over the bladder. The pubic symphysis is identified as a clear white dense structure located transversely, serving as a major landmark in the procedure.

The surgeon exposes Cooper's ligament, located at the upper edge of the pubic bone, by dissecting adjacent tissues. The grasper in the left hand is used to bluntly press down the bladder, stretching the tissue fibers between the bladder wall and the pubic symphysis. These fibers are then carefully cut under full visual inspection. The pubic symphysis is visible as a small protrusion, serving as a landmark for further dissection.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the hernial sac and internal ring after completing the initial dissection of the lateral Hasselbach space and the medial Retzius space.

The surgeon isolates individual connective tissue fibers between the peritoneum and the abdominal wall, carefully cutting them using electrocautery. The parietal peritoneum is preserved to avoid additional suturing later. The lower flap of the peritoneum is stretched to facilitate sliding the tissues towards the abdominal wall. The dissection around the inguinal ring is shown, highlighting the ease of separation in a medial direct hernia compared to a lateral oblique hernia.

The surgeon cuts the stretched tissues of the pre-transversalis fascia, releasing the peritoneum from the vas deferens below. The dissection of the flap becomes easier, allowing for clearer separation of the direct hernia contents. Solid traction behind the fatty tissue helps assess the boundaries of the hernia defect, revealing the white compact fibrous connective tissue of the true hernia defect ring.

The surgeon applies traction to adipose tissue around the hernia defect, revealing a narrow mouth filled with adipose tissue. As the tissues are released, the complete circumference of the hernia defect becomes gradually visible.

The surgeon releases additional adipose tissue adhesions on the lower edge of the hernia defect, carefully controlling the network of small vessels in the area. The vessels are isolated and coagulated, allowing full visualization of Cooper's ligament.

This material is a presentation of a full recording of this procedure, and only the short parts with cleaning the optics lens have been removed from the video. The playback rate is one point five times faster than an original speed of the procedure, so you can see how much time is actually needed for the following steps of this surgery.

The surgeon carefully separates tissues on the lateral edge of the direct hernia, taking care to avoid damaging the nearby inferior epigastric vessels. The entire perimeter of the abdominal wall defect within the medial hernia is clearly visible, along with the cleansed branch of the pubic bone. The surgeon begins further separation of tissues between the bladder and the external iliac vein, preparing to place the lower edge of the mesh without risk of folding.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lower peritoneal flap in its lateral part, creating space for the lower edge of the mesh. The peritoneum is carefully separated from the testicular vessels, which are gradually and gently slid away. Near the vas deferens, a thin layer of pretransverse fascia is sharply cut, releasing the vas deferens for further separation.

Please note that although we present you with a complete recording of this operation and the progression of the surgery so far actually lasted twelve minutes, the surgeon still uses only the same two instruments and has not changed them and only the grasper and tip of scissors were sufficient to separate the tissues. However, it should be emphasized that, especially in the case of poorly dissecting tissues and in the case of lateral hernias, there is often a need for additional grasper or dissector, and the separation of tissues is then more often done bluntly. One should also make sure that the medial space is properly dissected.

The surgeon grasps the pre-bladder adipose tissue and pulls it medially away from the external iliac vein. This maneuver helps identify the thinnest area of adipose tissue for separation. Cauterization is performed on the fibers before cutting to prevent fluid accumulation from small lymph vessels. This process is repeated to gradually free the space.

The surgeon releases the upper flap of the peritoneum to create a small pocket for the superior edge of the synthetic mesh. Using two instruments, the peritoneum is grasped and pulled tangentially to the abdominal cavity surface, separating it from the abdominal wall. The flap is gradually moved medially, passing it between instruments, and the synthetic mesh is inserted into the preperitoneal pocket.

The surgeon positions a lightweight three-dimensional polypropylene mesh over the narrow orifice of a direct hernia, ensuring appropriate margins around the defect. The lower edge of the mesh is carefully placed to prevent folding by the peritoneum or urinary bladder. The lower flap of the peritoneum is lifted upwards without disturbing the mesh position, and the hernial sac peritoneum is pulled out to prevent internal strangulation.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum using an absorbable suture, completing the procedure. Gas is desquamated from the preperitoneal space to ensure the mesh remains in its correct position without shifting.
Thank you for watching. We invite you to check out our other videos and we encourage you to subscribe to this channel.
